hazrd 0.2.1
  • Get started
  • Reference
  • Changelog

NA

Source: .github/copilot-instructions.md

Quick orientation

This repository is an R package named hazrd focused on evaluating and visualizing polygenic hazard scores (PHS) using survival models. Key components live in the R/ directory and tests use testthat/.

Files to scan first - R/phs_metrics.R — user-facing metric orchestrator (HR, C-index, OR) - R/metrics-internal.R — metric implementations (._calc_* functions) - R/bootstrap-internal.R — bootstrapping orchestration; uses boot::boot - R/create_lookup.R — generation of per-percentile K-M predictions - tests/testthat/ — unit tests to validate behavior - DESCRIPTION, NAMESPACE, README.Rmd — packaging and install metadata - Never edit README.md directly — it is auto-generated by knitting README.Rmd via rmarkdown::render(). Always use the Docker container which has pandoc: docker run --rm -v /path/to/hazrd:/repo my-r-image \ Rscript -e 'rmarkdown::render("/repo/README.Rmd", output_file = "/repo/README.md")' Do NOT use knitr::knit() — it lacks pandoc and emits the YAML frontmatter as literal text in the output. - docs/specs/spec-sheet.md — package spec sheet (may be local/ignored; ask the maintainer for a copy)

Big-picture architecture & data flow - Input: a data.frame with at minimum phs, age (time) and status (event). - phs_metrics() is the single primary entry-point for computing metrics; it fits a Cox model (survival::coxph) and derives metrics from the fit. - Metric calculation is split: point estimates are computed first; if bootstrap = TRUE the package calls .run_bootstrap() which re-fits the model on resamples and derives bootstrap CIs using boot::boot + boot.ci. - Outputs are tidy tibbles with canonical columns: metric, estimate, conf_low, conf_high, se, n_numerator, n_denominator, method, adjusted.

Developer spec - See docs/specs/spec-sheet.md for the canonical package specification. NOTE: this file may be kept locally and git-ignored by the maintainer. If you cannot find it in the repo, ask the maintainer to provide the Markdown copy. The spec documents package goals, input conventions (string column names), metric naming conventions (e.g. HR[80-100]_[0-20]), bootstrap behavior (internal boot::boot usage and CI methods), and worked examples for phs_metrics, phs_km, and phs_abs_risk.

Project-specific conventions & patterns (important for edits) - Column names are usually passed as strings (e.g. phs = "phs", time = "age", event = "status"). Functions accept string names and often index the data with data[[phs]] rather than non-standard-eval. - HR metric naming convention is literal and parsed elsewhere: e.g. HR[80-100]_[0-20] — tests and bootstrapping rely on these exact names to align CI results back to rows. - Bootstrapping: parallel argument is present but only "no" is implemented (see .run_bootstrap() guard). Avoid changing parallel semantics without updating that guard and tests. - Many internal helpers return tibbles with the canonical column order; keep that contract when adding new metrics.

Build / test / debug workflows (commands you can run locally) - Install dev version: in R: devtools::install("/path/to/hazrd") or devtools::build() then install.packages(..., repos = NULL, type = "source"). - Run package tests: in R: devtools::test() or from shell: R -e ‘devtools::test()’ - Run R CMD check: from repo root: R CMD check –as-cran . - The repo has a GitHub Action R-CMD-check.yaml (see badge in README.md) — mimic the action’s R versions if reproducing CI locally.

Integration points & external deps - Declared imports in DESCRIPTION: boot, dplyr, ggplot2, survival, tibble, tidyr, scales. Use those for consistent idioms. - boot::boot and boot::boot.ci are central — changes to the bootstrap interface must preserve the numeric-vector contract used by boot.

Testing & safety notes for code changes - Add unit tests to tests/testthat/ for any change in metric logic or bootstrap behavior. The package uses testthat edition 3. - Keep the reported metric names stable: tests and bootstrap matching rely on exact metric name strings. - When modifying exported functions, update roxygen comments in R/ source — run devtools::document() to regenerate NAMESPACE and man/ files. - Never use \% in roxygen #' comments — the backslash doubles in the generated Rd (\\%), where % then starts an Rd comment and can corrupt the \arguments{} block. Use plain text (e.g. “percent” or “0-20”) instead.

Pre-merge checklist (dev -> main)

This is the full workflow from issue to merged release. Work is done on dev; main should always be clean and passing.

1. During development (on dev)

2. Documentation (on dev, before merge)

    • Never use \% in roxygen comments — use plain text (“percent” or “0-20”) instead; see safety notes above

  • docker run --rm -v /path/to/hazrd:/repo my-r-image \
  Rscript -e 'rmarkdown::render("/repo/README.Rmd", output_file = "/repo/README.md")'

    Commit the updated README.md

3. Final checks (on dev, before merge)

4. Merge and tag

5. Post-merge

Good example edits to get started - Small bugfix: ensure or_age bounds are handled (see .calc_or_metric) — add test covering an or_age outside observed range. - Add a new C-index method: implement "uno" in .calc_cindex_metric() and add a test and example in R/phs_metrics.R documentation.

Prompting hints for the AI agent - When you change metric output shape, list the exact column names and show a one-row example tibble so maintainers can verify compatibility. - For bootstrap changes, include a minimal reproducible call of phs_metrics with bootstrap = TRUE and n_boot = 50 in tests so CI runs fast.

If anything in this file is unclear or you’d like more detail (examples of expected inputs, a sample dev/test script, or CI matrix notes), tell me which section and I’ll expand it.

On this page
